item.title: FICAC witness, Ravu refuses to reveal names on the stand
item.description: In an unexpected development in the trial of former Deputy Prime Minister Manoa Kamikamica, the Fiji Independent Commission Against Corruption's second witness has refused to reveal who questioned him about his case. Send your NEWS TIPS and VIDEOS to us on our Facebook or Instagram OR you can email us on tips@fijivillage.com.fj
item.content:encoded:
In an unexpected development in the trial of former Deputy Prime Minister Manoa Kamikamica, the Fiji Independent Commission Against Corruption's second witness has refused to reveal who questioned him about his case.

item.title: Collective action needed to strengthen Fiji's defence against scams – Consumer Council CEO
item.description: The Consumer Council of Fiji is calling for stronger collaboration across government, businesses and the wider community to combat the growing threat of scams in the country. Speaking at the launch of National Scams Awareness Week 2026 at the Grand Pacific Hotel today, Consumer Council of Fiji CEO Seema Shandil stressed that awareness alone is no longer enough to protect consumers in an increasing
item.content:encoded:
The Consumer Council of Fiji is calling for stronger collaboration across government, businesses and the wider community to combat the growing threat of scams in the country.

Speaking at the launch of National Scams Awareness Week 2026 at the Grand Pacific Hotel today, Consumer Council of Fiji CEO Seema Shandil stressed that awareness alone is no longer enough to protect consumers in an increasingly digital world.

Shandil says Fiji must move beyond simply raising awareness and focus on building stronger systems, partnerships and consumer protections to safeguard people from evolving scam tactics.

She acknowledged the continued support of partners and stakeholders over the past three years, saying the fight against scams requires a whole-of-government and whole-of-society approach.

The theme "Building a National Defense Against Scams," NSAW 2026 reaffirms the commitment to empowering consumers and creating a safer digital environment for all Fijians.

Send your NEWS TIPS and VIDEOS to us on our Facebook or Instagram OR you can email us on tips@fijivillage.com.fj

item.title: Gallau, Rakiraki farmers choose harvesting over strike despite challenges
item.description: A Rakiraki sugarcane farmer says growers in Gallau would rather harvest their cane than go on strike because they would ultimately be the ones to suffer the losses. Speaking on farmers' demand for an increase in the guaranteed cane price from $85 to $110 per tonne, former Advisory Councillor and Rakiraki sugarcane farmer Shri Krishna says the increase would be helpful.
item.content:encoded:
A Rakiraki sugarcane farmer says growers in Gallau would rather harvest their cane than go on strike because they would ultimately be the ones to suffer the losses.

Speaking on farmers' demand for an increase in the guaranteed cane price from $85 to $110 per tonne, former Advisory Councillor and Rakiraki sugarcane farmer Shri Krishna says the increase would be helpful.

However, he says farmers have no choice but to harvest their cane, otherwise it could go to waste or be damaged during the rainy season.

He says, in the end, harvesting is the only practical option.

Krishna says sugarcane farmers in Gallau, Rakiraki began harvesting as soon as the mills opened to ensure no cane is left unharvested.

He says farmers continue to face a number of challenges, including financial difficulties, a shortage of labour and the rising cost of fertiliser.

Krishna has thanks the Government for providing inmates to assist farmers with harvesting.

Insert: Krishna on assistance 22nd Hindi July

He says he does not rely solely on sugarcane farming for his livelihood, as he also grows rice, cassava, sweet potatoe, coriander and other vegetables adding he also makes sasa brooms and homemade ghee to supplement his income.

Send your NEWS TIPS and VIDEOS to us on our Facebook or Instagram OR you can email us on tips@fijivillage.com.fj

item.title: 95% student attendance recorded in Taveuni as bus services resume
item.description: Students in Taveuni have returned to school without further disruption after Taveuni Buses Limited resumed services across the island. Permanent Secretary for Education, Navin Raj says student attendance reached 95 percent today.
item.content:encoded:
Students in Taveuni have returned to school without further disruption after Taveuni Buses Limited resumed services across the island.

Permanent Secretary for Education, Navin Raj says student attendance reached 95 percent today.

He says the Year 5 and Year 7 Literacy and Numeracy Assessment also went ahead successfully, with 98 percent of students sitting the exams.

Students who missed yesterday's assessment will be able to sit it on the scheduled catch-up day.

Raj is encouraging all students to continue attending school as normal and says the Ministry will continue working closely with transport providers and relevant authorities to ensure students have reliable access to education.

He also thanked Taveuni Buses Limited, government agencies, temporary transport providers, school leaders, parents and community members who worked together to support students during the disruption.

Send your NEWS TIPS and VIDEOS to us on our Facebook or Instagram OR you can email us on tips@fijivillage.com.fj

item.title: FWCC urges senior police officers to strengthen survivor-centred response to violence against women
item.description: The Fiji Women's Crisis Centre has urged senior police officers to strengthen their response to cases of violence against women and girls by adopting a survivor-centred approach that prioritises the rights, safety, dignity and wellbeing of survivors. This was highlighted during a training session with 30 participants of the ongoing Superintendents Qualifying Course, comprising officers from the ra
item.content:encoded:
The Fiji Women's Crisis Centre has urged senior police officers to strengthen their response to cases of violence against women and girls by adopting a survivor-centred approach that prioritises the rights, safety, dignity and wellbeing of survivors.

This was highlighted during a training session with 30 participants of the ongoing Superintendents Qualifying Course, comprising officers from the ranks of Inspector to Superintendent.

FWCC Manager Legal Miliana Tarai and Counsellor Advocate Akisi Rokara led discussions on gender, violence against women and girls, human rights and the legal frameworks that guide police responses to such cases.

Discussion took place on the critical role police officers play in ensuring survivors have access to protection, support services and justice.

The FWCC facilitators also shared concerns and complaints received from survivors regarding police responses to cases of violence.

The participants also examined how unequal gender power relations contribute to violence and discrimination against women and girls.

They say the discussions allowed participants to reflect on how police attitudes, practices and responses can influence survivors' experiences and their confidence in seeking help.

FWCC says it wants to continue working closely with the Force and other stakeholders to strengthen institutional responses to violence against women and girls and promote a justice system that is accessible, accountable and responsive to the needs of survivors.

Send your NEWS TIPS and VIDEOS to us on our Facebook or Instagram OR you can email us on tips@fijivillage.com.fj

item.title: Children with disabilities continue to face barriers to education
item.description: Many children with disabilities continue to struggle to access education because of transport difficulties, inaccessible school environments, discrimination, high support needs, a lack of specialist support, limited access to special schools outside urban areas and unstable home environments. This has been highlighted in the Rights of Children with Disabilities Report.
item.content:encoded:
Many children with disabilities continue to struggle to access education because of transport difficulties, inaccessible school environments, discrimination, high support needs, a lack of specialist support, limited access to special schools outside urban areas and unstable home environments.

This has been highlighted in the Rights of Children with Disabilities Report.

The report says more students with disabilities are now enrolled in mainstream schools than in special schools, with 1,867 attending mainstream schools compared with 1,285 in special schools.

This highlights the growing impact of efforts to promote inclusive education in Fiji.

The report highlights that there have been important advances in inclusive education, including the implementation of the Special and Inclusive Education Policy, grants for accommodations and assistive devices, and ongoing teacher training initiatives.

Parents told researchers that their children enjoy attending school, have a strong desire to learn, and deserve the same right to education as every other child, stressing that school helps children develop valuable social skills and a sense of belonging.

According to the report, there are 326 mainstream primary schools and 139 mainstream secondary schools in Fiji that enrol students with disabilities.

Students with disabilities have also been supported through measures such as enlarged-print learning materials, modified lessons, sign language interpreters, and teacher aide support.

Some schools have improved accessibility by building ramps and railings, providing transport for students, and introducing inclusive classroom technology.

Despite these improvements, the report says children with higher support needs continue to face the greatest challenges, with some never attending school, starting school late, leaving early, or missing out on vocational education opportunities altogether.

It also reveals a significant gender gap, with almost twice as many boys with disabilities enrolled in both mainstream and special schools as girls, unlike the general student population, where enrolment is almost evenly split between boys and girls.

While many teachers have been praised for supporting children with disabilities, the report says others have displayed discriminatory attitudes or focused more on high-achieving students without disabilities.

It says that while 115 teachers have specialist qualifications in special education, many have not received the training needed to adequately support children with disabilities.

The report also raises concerns that some mainstream schools are encouraging parents to enrol their children in special schools because they believe they are unable to provide the necessary support.

Send your NEWS TIPS and VIDEOS to us on our Facebook or Instagram OR you can email us on tips@fijivillage.com.fj

item.title: Next General Election could be held under a new Constitution - Turaga
item.description: The next General Election could be held under a new Constitution, with Acting Attorney General Siromi Turaga saying it is possible
